questions.promptTex: \text{A radius and its tangent line meet at the point of tangency. What angle do they form?}
questions.explanation: The tangent at a point is perpendicular to the radius drawn to that point: $90°$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Circle of radius 6. External point } E \text{ at distance 10 from center.} \\ \text{Find the tangent length from } E \text{ to the circle.}
questions.explanation: Right triangle with hypotenuse $OE = 10$, leg $r = 6$. Tangent leg = $\sqrt{100 - 36} = 8$.

steps.text: **Setup.** The triangle formed by center $O$, external point $E$, and point of tangency $T$ has a right angle at $T$.

questions.prompt: Two tangent segments are drawn from an external point E to a circle. What can you say about their lengths?
hints: Same in the incircle lesson too — tangent lengths from a vertex were equal.
questions.explanation: The two tangent segments from an external point to a circle have equal length. This follows from the two right triangles being congruent (HL: common hypotenuse OE, equal legs OT₁ = OT₂ = r).

questions.promptTex: \text{Two tangents from external point } E \text{ touch a circle at } T_1, T_2. \\ \angle T_1 E T_2 = 50°. \text{ Find central angle } \angle T_1 O T_2.
hints: Quadrilateral $ET_1OT_2$ has two right angles (at the tangent points).
Angles in a quadrilateral sum to $360°$. So $\angle E + \angle O + 90° + 90° = 360°$.
questions.explanation: In quadrilateral $ET_1OT_2$ the angles at $T_1, T_2$ are both $90°$, so $\angle E + \angle O = 360 - 180 = 180°$. Thus $\angle O = 180 - 50 = 130°$.

steps.text: **Approach.** Quadrilateral $ET_1OT_2$ has two right angles (at the tangent points).
Angles in a quadrilateral sum to $360°$. So $\angle E + \angle O + 90° + 90° = 360°$.
**Solution.** In quadrilateral $ET_1OT_2$ the angles at $T_1, T_2$ are both $90°$, so $\angle E + \angle O = 360 - 180 = 180°$. Thus $\angle O = 180 - 50 = 130°$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Circle has radius } r. \, OE = 2r. \, \text{Find the tangent length from } E.

questions.prompt: A line passes through the center of a circle. Where (if anywhere) can it be tangent to the circle?
choices: At one point on the circle
Nowhere — it must be a secant (chord) through the center
hints: A line through the center intersects the circle at TWO points.
Tangent means touches at one point only.
questions.explanation: A line through the center intersects the circle at two diametrically opposite points — it's a *diameter*, not a tangent.

questions.promptTex: \text{Two circles externally tangent to each other have radii } 3 \text{ and } 5. \\ \text{Find the distance between their centers.}
hints: Externally tangent circles touch at one point on the line connecting their centers.
Distance between centers = sum of radii.
questions.explanation: When two circles are externally tangent, the distance between centers equals the sum of radii: $3 + 5 = 8$.

questions.prompt: Why is tangency special among 'line meets circle' situations?
choices: It's the case of exactly one intersection point (the line neither cuts through nor misses).
It's when the line passes through the center.
It's when the line is shorter than the diameter.
It's when the radius equals the tangent length.
hints: A line and a circle can meet 0, 1, or 2 times.
questions.explanation: A generic line either misses a circle (0 intersections) or cuts through it as a secant (2 intersections). Tangent is the borderline: exactly 1 intersection. This makes tangents the 'limiting' case of secants — a useful idea in calculus too (tangent line = limit of secant lines).
